titleHtml: Unit 3: Energy is conserved and its transformation affects living things and the environment
title: Unit 3: Energy is conserved and its transformation affects living things and the environment

chapters.titleHtml: Chapter 3.1: What are the properties of energy?
chapters.title: Chapter 3.1: What are the properties of energy?

concepts.titleHtml: 3.1 Concept 1: Energy can produce change in a system — p.200–201
concepts.title: 3.1 Concept 1: Energy can produce change in a system — p.200–201

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Energy as an indirect concept
kps.title: KP1: Energy as an indirect concept
kps.coreHtml: Energy cannot be observed directly, but its effects can be observed.
kps.core: Energy cannot be observed directly, but its effects can be observed.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify energy by the changes it causes in objects, motion, temperature, sound, or position.
kps.application: Identify energy by the changes it causes in objects, motion, temperature, sound, or position.

kps.title: KP2: Energy and change
kps.coreHtml: Energy can cause change in a system.
kps.core: Energy can cause change in a system.
kps.applicationHtml: Use observed change as evidence that energy is present or being used.
kps.application: Use observed change as evidence that energy is present or being used.

kps.title: KP3: System
kps.coreHtml: A system is anything under observation.
kps.core: A system is anything under observation.
kps.applicationHtml: Define the object, process, or group being studied before analyzing energy.
kps.application: Define the object, process, or group being studied before analyzing energy.

kps.title: KP4: Surroundings
kps.coreHtml: The surroundings include everything outside the system.
kps.core: The surroundings include everything outside the system.
kps.applicationHtml: Separate the system from the environment when tracking energy changes.
kps.application: Separate the system from the environment when tracking energy changes.

kps.title: KP5: Properties of energy
kps.coreHtml: Energy can take different forms, be transferred or transformed, and depend on different physical quantities.
kps.core: Energy can take different forms, be transferred or transformed, and depend on different physical quantities.
kps.applicationHtml: Use these properties to organize energy observations in real situations.
kps.application: Use these properties to organize energy observations in real situations.

concepts.titleHtml: 3.1 Concept 2: There are different forms of energy — p.202–205
concepts.title: 3.1 Concept 2: There are different forms of energy — p.202–205

kps.title: KP1: Kinetic energy
kps.coreHtml: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion.
kps.core: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify moving objects, moving particles, moving electrons, and vibrations as examples of kinetic energy.
kps.application: Identify moving objects, moving particles, moving electrons, and vibrations as examples of kinetic energy.

kps.title: KP2: Potential energy
kps.coreHtml: Potential energy is stored energy due to an object’s condition or position.
kps.core: Potential energy is stored energy due to an object’s condition or position.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify stored energy in height, chemical bonds, stretched objects, separated charges, magnets, or atomic nuclei.
kps.application: Identify stored energy in height, chemical bonds, stretched objects, separated charges, magnets, or atomic nuclei.

kps.title: KP3: Mechanical kinetic energy
kps.coreHtml: Mechanical kinetic energy is the energy of motion of objects larger than atoms and molecules.
kps.core: Mechanical kinetic energy is the energy of motion of objects larger than atoms and molecules.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify moving bicycles, balls, vehicles, or people as systems with mechanical kinetic energy.
kps.application: Identify moving bicycles, balls, vehicles, or people as systems with mechanical kinetic energy.

kps.title: KP4: Thermal energy and heat
kps.coreHtml: Thermal energy is the random motion of particles; heat is thermal energy transferred from one object to another.
kps.core: Thermal energy is the random motion of particles; heat is thermal energy transferred from one object to another.
kps.applicationHtml: Distinguish thermal energy from heat in scientific language.
kps.application: Distinguish thermal energy from heat in scientific language.

kps.titleHtml: KP5: Radiant, electrical kinetic, and sound energy
kps.title: KP5: Radiant, electrical kinetic, and sound energy
kps.coreHtml: Radiant energy travels as electromagnetic waves; electrical kinetic energy is moving electrons; sound energy is vibration of matter.
kps.core: Radiant energy travels as electromagnetic waves; electrical kinetic energy is moving electrons; sound energy is vibration of matter.
kps.applicationHtml: Classify light, current, lightning, and sound waves as forms of energy.
kps.application: Classify light, current, lightning, and sound waves as forms of energy.

kps.title: KP6: Forms of potential energy
kps.coreHtml: Gravitational, chemical, elastic, magnetic, electrical, and nuclear energy are forms of potential energy.
kps.core: Gravitational, chemical, elastic, magnetic, electrical, and nuclear energy are forms of potential energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Match stored energy examples to the correct potential energy category.
kps.application: Match stored energy examples to the correct potential energy category.

kps.title: KP7: Energy in multiple forms
kps.coreHtml: A system can have both kinetic energy and potential energy at the same time.
kps.core: A system can have both kinetic energy and potential energy at the same time.
kps.applicationHtml: Analyze moving objects above a reference point, such as a cyclist or roller coaster.
kps.application: Analyze moving objects above a reference point, such as a cyclist or roller coaster.

concepts.titleHtml: 3.1 Concept 3: Energy can be transferred or transformed — p.206–208
concepts.title: 3.1 Concept 3: Energy can be transferred or transformed — p.206–208

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Law of conservation of energy
kps.title: KP1: Law of conservation of energy
kps.coreHtml: Energy is neither created nor destroyed; it is transferred or transformed.
kps.core: Energy is neither created nor destroyed; it is transferred or transformed.
kps.applicationHtml: Track energy before and after an energy change.
kps.application: Track energy before and after an energy change.

kps.title: KP2: Energy transformation
kps.coreHtml: Energy transformation occurs when energy changes from one form to another.
kps.core: Energy transformation occurs when energy changes from one form to another.
kps.applicationHtml: Describe chemical potential energy changing into electrical, radiant, or thermal energy.
kps.application: Describe chemical potential energy changing into electrical, radiant, or thermal energy.

kps.title: KP3: Energy transfer
kps.coreHtml: Energy transfer occurs when energy moves from one object or place to another while staying in the same form.
kps.core: Energy transfer occurs when energy moves from one object or place to another while staying in the same form.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify energy moving between objects, such as one pool ball transferring mechanical kinetic energy to another.
kps.application: Identify energy moving between objects, such as one pool ball transferring mechanical kinetic energy to another.

kps.title: KP4: Useful and unusable energy
kps.coreHtml: No energy transformation is 100% efficient; some energy becomes less useful for the intended task.
kps.core: No energy transformation is 100% efficient; some energy becomes less useful for the intended task.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why thermal energy and sound energy often appear as byproducts.
kps.application: Explain why thermal energy and sound energy often appear as byproducts.

kps.title: KP5: Energy and system types
kps.coreHtml: Open systems exchange matter and energy; closed systems exchange energy but not matter; isolated systems exchange neither matter nor energy.
kps.core: Open systems exchange matter and energy; closed systems exchange energy but not matter; isolated systems exchange neither matter nor energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Classify systems by what can enter or leave them.
kps.application: Classify systems by what can enter or leave them.

kps.title: KP6: System-surroundings energy exchange
kps.coreHtml: When a system releases energy, the surroundings absorb it; when a system absorbs energy, the surroundings release it.
kps.core: When a system releases energy, the surroundings absorb it; when a system absorbs energy, the surroundings release it.
kps.applicationHtml: Track energy movement between a system and its surroundings.
kps.application: Track energy movement between a system and its surroundings.

kps.title: KP7: Transfer and transformation together
kps.coreHtml: Real situations often involve both energy transfer and energy transformation.
kps.core: Real situations often involve both energy transfer and energy transformation.
kps.applicationHtml: Analyze a catapult, light bulb, or pool-ball collision by naming both movement and form changes of energy.
kps.application: Analyze a catapult, light bulb, or pool-ball collision by naming both movement and form changes of energy.

concepts.titleHtml: 3.1 Concept 4: Different variables contribute to different forms of energy — p.209–214
concepts.title: 3.1 Concept 4: Different variables contribute to different forms of energy — p.209–214

kps.title: KP1: Physical quantities and energy
kps.coreHtml: Different physical quantities contribute to different forms of energy.
kps.core: Different physical quantities contribute to different forms of energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify which variables matter before comparing energy in two systems.
kps.application: Identify which variables matter before comparing energy in two systems.

kps.title: KP2: Mechanical kinetic energy variables
kps.coreHtml: Mechanical kinetic energy depends on mass and velocity.
kps.core: Mechanical kinetic energy depends on mass and velocity.
kps.applicationHtml: Predict that greater mass or greater speed increases mechanical kinetic energy.
kps.application: Predict that greater mass or greater speed increases mechanical kinetic energy.

kps.title: KP3: Mechanical kinetic energy equation
kps.coreHtml: \(E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\), where \(E_k\) is mechanical kinetic energy, \(m\) is mass, and \(v\) is velocity.
kps.core: \(E k = 1 2 mv 2\), where \(E k\) is mechanical kinetic energy, \(m\) is mass, and \(v\) is velocity.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why doubling velocity has a larger effect than doubling mass.
kps.application: Explain why doubling velocity has a larger effect than doubling mass.

kps.title: KP4: Gravitational potential energy variables
kps.coreHtml: Gravitational potential energy depends on mass, gravitational field strength, and change in height from a reference point.
kps.core: Gravitational potential energy depends on mass, gravitational field strength, and change in height from a reference point.
kps.applicationHtml: Predict how height, mass, and gravity affect stored gravitational energy.
kps.application: Predict how height, mass, and gravity affect stored gravitational energy.

kps.title: KP5: Gravitational potential energy equation
kps.coreHtml: \(E_g = mg\Delta h\), where \(E_g\) is gravitational potential energy, \(m\) is mass, \(g\) is acceleration due to gravity, and \(\Delta h\) is change in height.
kps.core: \(E g = mg h\), where \(E g\) is gravitational potential energy, \(m\) is mass, \(g\) is acceleration due to gravity, and \( h\) is change in height.
kps.applicationHtml: Relate gravitational potential energy to falling objects, hills, cliffs, and amusement rides.
kps.application: Relate gravitational potential energy to falling objects, hills, cliffs, and amusement rides.

kps.title: KP6: Reference position
kps.coreHtml: Gravitational potential energy is measured relative to a chosen reference position.
kps.core: Gravitational potential energy is measured relative to a chosen reference position.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why the same object can have different \(E_g\) values depending on the reference point.
kps.application: Explain why the same object can have different \(E g\) values depending on the reference point.

kps.titleHtml: KP7: Friction and apparent energy loss
kps.title: KP7: Friction and apparent energy loss
kps.coreHtml: Friction transforms mechanical energy into thermal energy and sound energy.
kps.core: Friction transforms mechanical energy into thermal energy and sound energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why a sled does not return to the same height even though total energy is conserved.
kps.application: Explain why a sled does not return to the same height even though total energy is conserved.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-A: Build a Rube Goldberg Machine — p.216
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-A: Build a Rube Goldberg Machine — p.216

kps.title: KP1: Chain of energy changes
kps.coreHtml: A Rube Goldberg machine uses a sequence of energy transfers and transformations to complete a task.
kps.core: A Rube Goldberg machine uses a sequence of energy transfers and transformations to complete a task.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify how one energy change triggers the next part of a system.
kps.application: Identify how one energy change triggers the next part of a system.

kps.title: KP2: System design
kps.coreHtml: The design of a system affects how energy is transferred and transformed.
kps.core: The design of a system affects how energy is transferred and transformed.
kps.applicationHtml: Improve a machine by reducing unwanted energy losses or making transfers more reliable.
kps.application: Improve a machine by reducing unwanted energy losses or making transfers more reliable.

kps.title: KP3: Energy evidence
kps.coreHtml: Motion, sound, height change, collision, and deformation can provide evidence of energy transfer or transformation.
kps.core: Motion, sound, height change, collision, and deformation can provide evidence of energy transfer or transformation.
kps.applicationHtml: Record visible and audible evidence to explain how the machine works.
kps.application: Record visible and audible evidence to explain how the machine works.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-B: Investigating Energy Transformation and Transfer — p.217
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-B: Investigating Energy Transformation and Transfer — p.217

kps.title: KP1: Observing energy transformation
kps.coreHtml: Energy transformation can be investigated by observing how a system changes form or motion.
kps.core: Energy transformation can be investigated by observing how a system changes form or motion.
kps.applicationHtml: Describe how energy changes form during a hands-on system.
kps.application: Describe how energy changes form during a hands-on system.

kps.title: KP2: Observing energy transfer
kps.coreHtml: Energy transfer can be investigated by observing how energy moves between parts of a system or to surroundings.
kps.core: Energy transfer can be investigated by observing how energy moves between parts of a system or to surroundings.
kps.applicationHtml: Track where energy moves during an investigation.
kps.application: Track where energy moves during an investigation.

kps.title: KP3: Variables affecting energy
kps.coreHtml: Design variables can change how much energy is transferred or transformed.
kps.core: Design variables can change how much energy is transferred or transformed.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify which design changes affect system performance.
kps.application: Identify which design changes affect system performance.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-C: Roller Coaster Physics — p.218–219
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-C: Roller Coaster Physics — p.218–219

kps.title: KP1: Roller coaster energy changes
kps.coreHtml: Roller coasters transform gravitational potential energy into mechanical kinetic energy and back again.
kps.core: Roller coasters transform gravitational potential energy into mechanical kinetic energy and back again.
kps.applicationHtml: Analyze energy changes at the top, bottom, and middle of hills.
kps.application: Analyze energy changes at the top, bottom, and middle of hills.

kps.title: KP2: Track height and speed
kps.coreHtml: Higher positions have more gravitational potential energy, while faster motion has more mechanical kinetic energy.
kps.core: Higher positions have more gravitational potential energy, while faster motion has more mechanical kinetic energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Predict where a coaster has maximum \(E_g\) and maximum \(E_k\).
kps.application: Predict where a coaster has maximum \(E g\) and maximum \(E k\).

kps.title: KP3: Friction and realistic motion
kps.coreHtml: Friction transforms some mechanical energy into thermal and sound energy.
kps.core: Friction transforms some mechanical energy into thermal and sound energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why real roller coasters cannot keep reaching the same height without added energy.
kps.application: Explain why real roller coasters cannot keep reaching the same height without added energy.

concepts.titleHtml: 3.2 Concept 1: Energy is transformed in chemical reactions — p.222–227
concepts.title: 3.2 Concept 1: Energy is transformed in chemical reactions — p.222–227

kps.title: KP1: Chemical potential energy
kps.coreHtml: Chemical reactions can transform energy stored in chemical bonds into other forms of energy.
kps.core: Chemical reactions can transform energy stored in chemical bonds into other forms of energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify chemical potential energy as the stored energy source in fuels, food, and biological molecules.
kps.application: Identify chemical potential energy as the stored energy source in fuels, food, and biological molecules.

kps.titleHtml: KP2: System and surroundings in chemical reactions
kps.title: KP2: System and surroundings in chemical reactions
kps.coreHtml: In a chemical reaction, the reacting materials are the system, and energy is released to or absorbed from the surroundings.
kps.core: In a chemical reaction, the reacting materials are the system, and energy is released to or absorbed from the surroundings.
kps.applicationHtml: Track whether energy moves from the system to the surroundings or from the surroundings into the system.
kps.application: Track whether energy moves from the system to the surroundings or from the surroundings into the system.

kps.titleHtml: KP3: Bond energy and reaction energy
kps.title: KP3: Bond energy and reaction energy
kps.coreHtml: The amount of energy transformed depends on the nature of the chemical bonds in the reactants and products.
kps.core: The amount of energy transformed depends on the nature of the chemical bonds in the reactants and products.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare reactant and product chemical potential energy to predict energy release or absorption.
kps.application: Compare reactant and product chemical potential energy to predict energy release or absorption.

kps.title: KP4: Exothermic and endothermic reactions
kps.coreHtml: If reactants have higher chemical potential energy than products, the reaction is exothermic; if reactants have lower chemical potential energy than products, the reaction is endothermic.
kps.core: If reactants have higher chemical potential energy than products, the reaction is exothermic; if reactants have lower chemical potential energy than products, the reaction is endothermic.
kps.applicationHtml: Classify reactions by comparing energy stored before and after the reaction.
kps.application: Classify reactions by comparing energy stored before and after the reaction.

kps.title: KP5: Cellular respiration
kps.coreHtml: Cellular respiration transforms chemical potential energy in glucose into ATP and other useful energy forms: \(C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{energy}\).
kps.core: Cellular respiration transforms chemical potential energy in glucose into ATP and other useful energy forms: \(C 6H 12 O 6 + 6O 2 6CO 2 + 6H 2O + energy \).
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how living things release energy from glucose in controlled steps instead of by burning.
kps.application: Explain how living things release energy from glucose in controlled steps instead of by burning.

kps.title: KP6: Photosynthesis
kps.coreHtml: Photosynthesis transforms light energy into chemical potential energy stored in glucose: \(6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{energy} \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2\).
kps.core: Photosynthesis transforms light energy into chemical potential energy stored in glucose: \(6CO 2 + 6H 2O + energy C 6H 12 O 6 + 6O 2\).
kps.applicationHtml: Connect chlorophyll, light energy, ATP, and glucose production in plants.
kps.application: Connect chlorophyll, light energy, ATP, and glucose production in plants.

kps.titleHtml: KP7: Fossil fuels and fuel cells
kps.title: KP7: Fossil fuels and fuel cells
kps.coreHtml: Fossil fuels store chemical potential energy from ancient solar energy, while fuel cells transform chemical potential energy directly into electrical energy.
kps.core: Fossil fuels store chemical potential energy from ancient solar energy, while fuel cells transform chemical potential energy directly into electrical energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare combustion of fossil fuels with hydrogen fuel cells in terms of energy transformation and pollution.
kps.application: Compare combustion of fossil fuels with hydrogen fuel cells in terms of energy transformation and pollution.

concepts.titleHtml: 3.2 Concept 2: Energy is transformed in nuclear reactions — p.228–232
concepts.title: 3.2 Concept 2: Energy is transformed in nuclear reactions — p.228–232

kps.title: KP1: Nuclear reactions
kps.coreHtml: Nuclear reactions involve changes in atomic nuclei and transform much more energy than chemical reactions.
kps.core: Nuclear reactions involve changes in atomic nuclei and transform much more energy than chemical reactions.
kps.applicationHtml: Distinguish chemical reactions, which rearrange atoms, from nuclear reactions, which change nuclei.
kps.application: Distinguish chemical reactions, which rearrange atoms, from nuclear reactions, which change nuclei.

kps.title: KP2: Isotopes and radioactive isotopes
kps.coreHtml: Isotopes have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons; unstable isotopes are radioactive.
kps.core: Isotopes have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons; unstable isotopes are radioactive.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why some nuclei emit particles or radiation to become more stable.
kps.application: Explain why some nuclei emit particles or radiation to become more stable.

kps.title: KP3: Nuclear decay and radiation
kps.coreHtml: Nuclear decay is a change to an atom due to emission of particles or radiation; radiation transfers energy as electromagnetic waves.
kps.core: Nuclear decay is a change to an atom due to emission of particles or radiation; radiation transfers energy as electromagnetic waves.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect radioactive decay to energy release from unstable nuclei.
kps.application: Connect radioactive decay to energy release from unstable nuclei.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Alpha, beta, and gamma decay
kps.title: KP4: Alpha, beta, and gamma decay
kps.coreHtml: Alpha decay emits a helium nucleus; beta decay emits a fast-moving electron; gamma decay emits a high-energy photon.
kps.core: Alpha decay emits a helium nucleus; beta decay emits a fast-moving electron; gamma decay emits a high-energy photon.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare how each decay type changes, or does not change, the atomic number and mass number.
kps.application: Compare how each decay type changes, or does not change, the atomic number and mass number.

kps.title: KP5: Nuclear fission
kps.coreHtml: Nuclear fission splits a heavy nucleus into smaller nuclei with the release of energy.
kps.core: Nuclear fission splits a heavy nucleus into smaller nuclei with the release of energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Describe how uranium-235 can split after absorbing a neutron.
kps.application: Describe how uranium-235 can split after absorbing a neutron.

kps.titleHtml: KP6: Chain reactions and nuclear reactors
kps.title: KP6: Chain reactions and nuclear reactors
kps.coreHtml: A chain reaction occurs when neutrons from one fission reaction trigger more fission reactions.
kps.core: A chain reaction occurs when neutrons from one fission reaction trigger more fission reactions.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how reactors control fission energy and transform thermal energy into electrical energy.
kps.application: Explain how reactors control fission energy and transform thermal energy into electrical energy.

kps.title: KP7: Nuclear fusion
kps.coreHtml: Nuclear fusion combines very small nuclei into a larger nucleus and releases energy.
kps.core: Nuclear fusion combines very small nuclei into a larger nucleus and releases energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare fusion in the Sun with fission in reactors, including energy per unit mass and radioactive waste.
kps.application: Compare fusion in the Sun with fission in reactors, including energy per unit mass and radioactive waste.

concepts.titleHtml: 3.2 Concept 3: Energy is transformed when light energy interacts with matter — p.234–235
concepts.title: 3.2 Concept 3: Energy is transformed when light energy interacts with matter — p.234–235

kps.title: KP1: Absorption
kps.coreHtml: Absorption occurs when energy is taken up by matter without being reflected or transmitted.
kps.core: Absorption occurs when energy is taken up by matter without being reflected or transmitted.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify absorption as the first step in many light-energy transformations.
kps.application: Identify absorption as the first step in many light-energy transformations.

kps.title: KP2: Excited electrons
kps.coreHtml: When matter absorbs light energy, electrons can move to higher energy levels.
kps.core: When matter absorbs light energy, electrons can move to higher energy levels.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how excited electrons allow light energy to become another form of energy.
kps.application: Explain how excited electrons allow light energy to become another form of energy.

kps.title: KP3: Chlorophyll and high-energy bonds
kps.coreHtml: Chlorophyll absorbs light energy, and this energy can be used to form high-energy chemical bonds.
kps.core: Chlorophyll absorbs light energy, and this energy can be used to form high-energy chemical bonds.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect light absorption to chemical potential energy storage in photosynthesis.
kps.application: Connect light absorption to chemical potential energy storage in photosynthesis.

kps.title: KP4: Photovoltaic cells
kps.coreHtml: Photovoltaic cells transform light energy directly into electrical kinetic energy.
kps.core: Photovoltaic cells transform light energy directly into electrical kinetic energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how absorbed light frees electrons and produces electric current.
kps.application: Explain how absorbed light frees electrons and produces electric current.

kps.title: KP5: Vision and rhodopsin
kps.coreHtml: Rhodopsin absorbs light energy, causing molecular changes that begin an electrical signal to the brain.
kps.core: Rhodopsin absorbs light energy, causing molecular changes that begin an electrical signal to the brain.
kps.applicationHtml: Trace light energy transformation from retina cells to nerve signals.
kps.application: Trace light energy transformation from retina cells to nerve signals.

kps.titleHtml: KP6: Light-energy transformation in different systems
kps.title: KP6: Light-energy transformation in different systems
kps.coreHtml: The way absorbed light energy is used depends on the properties and location of the absorbing molecule or material.
kps.core: The way absorbed light energy is used depends on the properties and location of the absorbing molecule or material.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare chlorophyll, photovoltaic cells, and human vision as examples of light-energy transformation.
kps.application: Compare chlorophyll, photovoltaic cells, and human vision as examples of light-energy transformation.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: Effects of Radiation on Living Tissues — p.233
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Effects of Radiation on Living Tissues — p.233

kps.title: KP1: Ionizing radiation
kps.coreHtml: Ionizing radiation can remove electrons from atoms and break chemical bonds.
kps.core: Ionizing radiation can remove electrons from atoms and break chemical bonds.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how radiation can damage molecules, cells, tissues, and organs.
kps.application: Explain how radiation can damage molecules, cells, tissues, and organs.

kps.title: KP2: Alpha radiation risk
kps.coreHtml: Alpha particles have high mass and charge, so they deposit energy near the surface they strike.
kps.core: Alpha particles have high mass and charge, so they deposit energy near the surface they strike.
kps.applicationHtml: Distinguish low external skin risk from high internal risk if alpha emitters are inhaled or ingested.
kps.application: Distinguish low external skin risk from high internal risk if alpha emitters are inhaled or ingested.

kps.title: KP3: Beta radiation risk
kps.coreHtml: Beta particles can penetrate the surface of matter and damage living tissue.
kps.core: Beta particles can penetrate the surface of matter and damage living tissue.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why beta radiation can cause serious skin burns and is more dangerous if inhaled or ingested.
kps.application: Explain why beta radiation can cause serious skin burns and is more dangerous if inhaled or ingested.

kps.title: KP4: Gamma radiation risk
kps.coreHtml: Gamma rays have no mass or charge and can pass deeply through dense matter before interacting.
kps.core: Gamma rays have no mass or charge and can pass deeply through dense matter before interacting.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why gamma radiation can ionize atoms anywhere in the body.
kps.application: Explain why gamma radiation can ionize atoms anywhere in the body.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Science at Work: Focus on Physics — p.236
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Science at Work: Focus on Physics — p.236

kps.title: KP1: Energy transformation careers
kps.coreHtml: Physics-related careers often require understanding how energy changes form or moves between systems.
kps.core: Physics-related careers often require understanding how energy changes form or moves between systems.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect energy transformation to work in audiology, radiation protection, biomedical engineering, oceanography, materials design, meteorology, and archaeology.
kps.application: Connect energy transformation to work in audiology, radiation protection, biomedical engineering, oceanography, materials design, meteorology, and archaeology.

kps.title: KP2: Energy tools and technologies
kps.coreHtml: Technologies such as radar, magnetometers, radiation protection systems, and weather instruments depend on energy transfer or transformation.
kps.core: Technologies such as radar, magnetometers, radiation protection systems, and weather instruments depend on energy transfer or transformation.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify how physics concepts support real-world investigation, safety, design, and prediction.
kps.application: Identify how physics concepts support real-world investigation, safety, design, and prediction.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take Action — Transforming Solar Energy to Make a Difference in Remote Areas — p.237
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take Action — Transforming Solar Energy to Make a Difference in Remote Areas — p.237

kps.title: KP1: Solar energy access
kps.coreHtml: Solar-energy technologies can help provide electricity, clean water, and emergency supplies in remote or underserved areas.
kps.core: Solar-energy technologies can help provide electricity, clean water, and emergency supplies in remote or underserved areas.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect energy transformation to practical needs in rural communities and disaster relief.
kps.application: Connect energy transformation to practical needs in rural communities and disaster relief.

kps.title: KP2: Solar-powered community technologies
kps.coreHtml: Solar devices can transform sunlight into electrical energy or use sunlight and gravity to support water filtration.
kps.core: Solar devices can transform sunlight into electrical energy or use sunlight and gravity to support water filtration.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare the Energy Hub and SunSaluter as technologies that use solar energy for community benefit.
kps.application: Compare the Energy Hub and SunSaluter as technologies that use solar energy for community benefit.

kps.title: KP3: Solarships and sustainable delivery
kps.coreHtml: Solarships combine buoyant lift, solar panels, and compact aircraft design to deliver supplies to remote areas.
kps.core: Solarships combine buoyant lift, solar panels, and compact aircraft design to deliver supplies to remote areas.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate how solar energy can reduce reliance on fossil fuels in transportation.
kps.application: Evaluate how solar energy can reduce reliance on fossil fuels in transportation.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: Storing Energy in Salt — p.238
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Storing Energy in Salt — p.238

kps.title: KP1: Solar energy storage problem
kps.coreHtml: Solar-to-electrical energy transformation is reduced at night and on cloudy days.
kps.core: Solar-to-electrical energy transformation is reduced at night and on cloudy days.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why large-scale solar power needs an energy-storage method.
kps.application: Explain why large-scale solar power needs an energy-storage method.

kps.titleHtml: KP2: Molten salt as thermal storage
kps.title: KP2: Molten salt as thermal storage
kps.coreHtml: Mirrors can focus sunlight onto a tower where molten salt absorbs solar energy and stores it as thermal energy.
kps.core: Mirrors can focus sunlight onto a tower where molten salt absorbs solar energy and stores it as thermal energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Trace energy transformation from sunlight to heated salt.
kps.application: Trace energy transformation from sunlight to heated salt.

kps.titleHtml: KP3: Stored thermal energy to electricity
kps.title: KP3: Stored thermal energy to electricity
kps.coreHtml: Heated molten salt can later boil water into steam that drives a turbine to generate electrical energy.
kps.core: Heated molten salt can later boil water into steam that drives a turbine to generate electrical energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how stored thermal energy can help a solar power plant operate when sunlight is unavailable.
kps.application: Explain how stored thermal energy can help a solar power plant operate when sunlight is unavailable.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-D: Solar Cooker Challenge — p.240
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-D: Solar Cooker Challenge — p.240

kps.title: KP1: Solar cooker design
kps.coreHtml: A solar cooker uses reflected and absorbed solar energy to heat water or food.
kps.core: A solar cooker uses reflected and absorbed solar energy to heat water or food.
kps.applicationHtml: Design a device that concentrates sunlight safely and effectively.
kps.application: Design a device that concentrates sunlight safely and effectively.

kps.title: KP2: Energy transfer and transformation
kps.coreHtml: Solar energy can be reflected, absorbed, transformed into thermal energy, and transferred to water.
kps.core: Solar energy can be reflected, absorbed, transformed into thermal energy, and transferred to water.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how cooker shape, material, orientation, and container design affect heating.
kps.application: Explain how cooker shape, material, orientation, and container design affect heating.

kps.title: KP3: Design testing and improvement
kps.coreHtml: Engineering design improves through testing, comparing results, and modifying variables.
kps.core: Engineering design improves through testing, comparing results, and modifying variables.
kps.applicationHtml: Use boiling time or temperature change as evidence for improving a solar cooker.
kps.application: Use boiling time or temperature change as evidence for improving a solar cooker.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-E: Fission versus Fusion — p.241
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-E: Fission versus Fusion — p.241

kps.title: KP1: Comparing nuclear processes
kps.coreHtml: Fission splits heavy nuclei, while fusion combines light nuclei.
kps.core: Fission splits heavy nuclei, while fusion combines light nuclei.
kps.applicationHtml: Create a clear comparison of reaction type, energy output, conditions, products, and waste.
kps.application: Create a clear comparison of reaction type, energy output, conditions, products, and waste.

kps.title: KP2: Nuclear energy decisions
kps.coreHtml: Fission is currently used for electrical energy generation because it can be controlled, while fusion remains difficult to control.
kps.core: Fission is currently used for electrical energy generation because it can be controlled, while fusion remains difficult to control.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why current nuclear power relies on fission rather than fusion.
kps.application: Explain why current nuclear power relies on fission rather than fusion.

kps.titleHtml: KP3: Public communication about nuclear energy
kps.title: KP3: Public communication about nuclear energy
kps.coreHtml: Scientific explanations of fission and fusion should be accurate, balanced, and appropriate for the audience.
kps.core: Scientific explanations of fission and fusion should be accurate, balanced, and appropriate for the audience.
kps.applicationHtml: Prepare educational material that compares benefits, risks, limitations, and future possibilities.
kps.application: Prepare educational material that compares benefits, risks, limitations, and future possibilities.

chapters.titleHtml: Chapter 3.3: How does energy transformation affect global systems?
chapters.title: Chapter 3.3: How does energy transformation affect global systems?

concepts.titleHtml: 3.3 Concept 1: Earth is a system in which energy is transformed — p.244–245
concepts.title: 3.3 Concept 1: Earth is a system in which energy is transformed — p.244–245

kps.title: KP1: Earth as a system
kps.coreHtml: Earth can be considered a system because its processes can be observed and analyzed together.
kps.core: Earth can be considered a system because its processes can be observed and analyzed together.
kps.applicationHtml: Define Earth, or part of Earth, as the system before tracking energy transformations.
kps.application: Define Earth, or part of Earth, as the system before tracking energy transformations.

kps.titleHtml: KP2: Energy transformations in Earth processes
kps.title: KP2: Energy transformations in Earth processes
kps.coreHtml: Earth’s wind, weather, tides, currents, volcanic activity, mountain building, and life processes depend on energy transformation.
kps.core: Earth’s wind, weather, tides, currents, volcanic activity, mountain building, and life processes depend on energy transformation.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect visible Earth processes to energy changing form.
kps.application: Connect visible Earth processes to energy changing form.

kps.titleHtml: KP3: Solar energy in Earth’s system
kps.title: KP3: Solar energy in Earth’s system
kps.coreHtml: Solar energy drives movement of water in the atmosphere, hydrosphere, and over land, and sustains most life on Earth.
kps.core: Solar energy drives movement of water in the atmosphere, hydrosphere, and over land, and sustains most life on Earth.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify solar energy as the largest energy input affecting Earth’s surface systems.
kps.application: Identify solar energy as the largest energy input affecting Earth’s surface systems.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Gravitational potential energy in Earth’s system
kps.title: KP4: Gravitational potential energy in Earth’s system
kps.coreHtml: Gravitational potential energy helps move water, including through tides caused by the Moon’s gravitational influence.
kps.core: Gravitational potential energy helps move water, including through tides caused by the Moon’s gravitational influence.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect tides and water movement to gravitational energy transformations.
kps.application: Connect tides and water movement to gravitational energy transformations.

kps.titleHtml: KP5: Nuclear energy in Earth’s system
kps.title: KP5: Nuclear energy in Earth’s system
kps.coreHtml: Nuclear energy from radioactive decay in Earth’s crust and mantle transforms into thermal energy.
kps.core: Nuclear energy from radioactive decay in Earth’s crust and mantle transforms into thermal energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect radioactive decay to volcanic activity, tectonic plate movement, geysers, and hot springs.
kps.application: Connect radioactive decay to volcanic activity, tectonic plate movement, geysers, and hot springs.

kps.title: KP6: Relative energy impact
kps.coreHtml: Solar energy transformation has the greatest impact on Earth’s system compared with gravitational and nuclear energy sources.
kps.core: Solar energy transformation has the greatest impact on Earth’s system compared with gravitational and nuclear energy sources.
kps.applicationHtml: Prioritize solar energy when explaining large-scale atmospheric, hydrosphere, and life-supporting processes.
kps.application: Prioritize solar energy when explaining large-scale atmospheric, hydrosphere, and life-supporting processes.

concepts.titleHtml: 3.3 Concept 2: Earth’s atmosphere is heated by the transformation and transfer of solar energy and thermal energy — p.246–249
concepts.title: 3.3 Concept 2: Earth’s atmosphere is heated by the transformation and transfer of solar energy and thermal energy — p.246–249

kps.title: KP1: Solar radiation
kps.coreHtml: Solar energy reaches Earth as electromagnetic radiation, including visible light, infrared radiation, and ultraviolet radiation.
kps.core: Solar energy reaches Earth as electromagnetic radiation, including visible light, infrared radiation, and ultraviolet radiation.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify incoming solar energy as radiation that can interact with air, water, land, and living things.
kps.application: Identify incoming solar energy as radiation that can interact with air, water, land, and living things.

kps.title: KP2: Absorption and reflection
kps.coreHtml: Some incoming solar energy is absorbed by Earth’s surface, while some is reflected back toward space.
kps.core: Some incoming solar energy is absorbed by Earth’s surface, while some is reflected back toward space.
kps.applicationHtml: Use absorption and reflection to explain why Earth does not absorb all incoming solar energy.
kps.application: Use absorption and reflection to explain why Earth does not absorb all incoming solar energy.

kps.title: KP3: Radiation from Earth’s surface
kps.coreHtml: Absorbed solar energy can be transformed into thermal energy and radiated again as infrared radiation.
kps.core: Absorbed solar energy can be transformed into thermal energy and radiated again as infrared radiation.
kps.applicationHtml: Trace energy from incoming sunlight to thermal energy leaving Earth’s surface.
kps.application: Trace energy from incoming sunlight to thermal energy leaving Earth’s surface.

kps.title: KP4: Greenhouse gases
kps.coreHtml: Greenhouse gases such as carbon dioxide, water vapour, nitrous oxide, and methane absorb infrared radiation.
kps.core: Greenhouse gases such as carbon dioxide, water vapour, nitrous oxide, and methane absorb infrared radiation.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how greenhouse gases help warm Earth’s atmosphere.
kps.application: Explain how greenhouse gases help warm Earth’s atmosphere.

kps.title: KP5: Greenhouse effect
kps.coreHtml: The greenhouse effect helps keep Earth’s average temperature moderate enough for life.
kps.core: The greenhouse effect helps keep Earth’s average temperature moderate enough for life.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why Earth would be much colder without naturally occurring greenhouse gases.
kps.application: Explain why Earth would be much colder without naturally occurring greenhouse gases.

kps.title: KP6: Conduction
kps.coreHtml: Conduction is the transfer of thermal energy between substances that are touching.
kps.core: Conduction is the transfer of thermal energy between substances that are touching.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how warm land or water transfers thermal energy to nearby air molecules.
kps.application: Explain how warm land or water transfers thermal energy to nearby air molecules.

kps.title: KP7: Convection
kps.coreHtml: Convection is the transfer of thermal energy by the movement of heated fluids, including gases and liquids.
kps.core: Convection is the transfer of thermal energy by the movement of heated fluids, including gases and liquids.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how warm, less-dense air rises and cooler, denser air sinks in the atmosphere.
kps.application: Explain how warm, less-dense air rises and cooler, denser air sinks in the atmosphere.

concepts.titleHtml: 3.3 Concept 3: Energy transfer and energy transformation moderate Earth’s temperature — p.250–251
concepts.title: 3.3 Concept 3: Energy transfer and energy transformation moderate Earth’s temperature — p.250–251

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Water cycle and energy movement
kps.title: KP1: Water cycle and energy movement
kps.coreHtml: The water cycle moves water and energy among the hydrosphere, geosphere, and atmosphere.
kps.core: The water cycle moves water and energy among the hydrosphere, geosphere, and atmosphere.
kps.applicationHtml: Track both water and energy through evaporation, transpiration, condensation, and precipitation.
kps.application: Track both water and energy through evaporation, transpiration, condensation, and precipitation.

kps.title: KP2: Evaporation
kps.coreHtml: During evaporation, solar energy is transformed into thermal energy as water molecules move faster and enter the atmosphere as vapour.
kps.core: During evaporation, solar energy is transformed into thermal energy as water molecules move faster and enter the atmosphere as vapour.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how evaporation moves thermal energy away from Earth’s surface.
kps.application: Explain how evaporation moves thermal energy away from Earth’s surface.

kps.title: KP3: Transpiration
kps.coreHtml: Transpiration releases water vapour from plants into the atmosphere and moves thermal energy with it.
kps.core: Transpiration releases water vapour from plants into the atmosphere and moves thermal energy with it.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect plant processes to energy movement in the atmosphere.
kps.application: Connect plant processes to energy movement in the atmosphere.

kps.title: KP4: Condensation
kps.coreHtml: As water vapour rises, cools, and condenses, thermal energy is released to the atmosphere.
kps.core: As water vapour rises, cools, and condenses, thermal energy is released to the atmosphere.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how cloud formation transfers energy to air molecules.
kps.application: Explain how cloud formation transfers energy to air molecules.

kps.title: KP5: Precipitation and gravitational energy
kps.coreHtml: During precipitation, gravitational potential energy transforms into mechanical kinetic energy as water falls and flows downhill.
kps.core: During precipitation, gravitational potential energy transforms into mechanical kinetic energy as water falls and flows downhill.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect rain, snow, runoff, and flowing water to energy transformation.
kps.application: Connect rain, snow, runoff, and flowing water to energy transformation.

kps.title: KP6: Specific heat capacity
kps.coreHtml: Specific heat capacity is the amount of energy needed to change the temperature of \(1\,g\) of a substance by \(1^\circ C\).
kps.core: Specific heat capacity is the amount of energy needed to change the temperature of \(1\,g\) of a substance by \(1 C\).
kps.applicationHtml: Use specific heat capacity to compare how quickly different materials warm or cool.
kps.application: Use specific heat capacity to compare how quickly different materials warm or cool.

kps.title: KP7: Oceans and temperature moderation
kps.coreHtml: Water has a high specific heat capacity, so oceans can store large amounts of thermal energy with small temperature changes.
kps.core: Water has a high specific heat capacity, so oceans can store large amounts of thermal energy with small temperature changes.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why coastal regions usually have cooler summers and warmer winters than inland regions.
kps.application: Explain why coastal regions usually have cooler summers and warmer winters than inland regions.

concepts.titleHtml: 3.3 Concept 4: Energy transformation and transfer can harm aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems — p.252–253
concepts.title: 3.3 Concept 4: Energy transformation and transfer can harm aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems — p.252–253

kps.title: KP1: Harmful energy transformations
kps.coreHtml: Energy transformations can harm ecosystems through natural events or human activities.
kps.core: Energy transformations can harm ecosystems through natural events or human activities.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare natural sources such as forest fires or volcanoes with human-caused impacts such as climate change or radiation exposure.
kps.application: Compare natural sources such as forest fires or volcanoes with human-caused impacts such as climate change or radiation exposure.

kps.title: KP2: Human-induced greenhouse effect
kps.coreHtml: Human activities add greenhouse gases to the atmosphere, increasing Earth’s average temperature.
kps.core: Human activities add greenhouse gases to the atmosphere, increasing Earth’s average temperature.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect electrical generation, vehicles, heating homes, and industry to increased greenhouse gas emissions.
kps.application: Connect electrical generation, vehicles, heating homes, and industry to increased greenhouse gas emissions.

kps.title: KP3: Aquatic ecosystems and temperature
kps.coreHtml: Aquatic organisms often thrive within specific temperature ranges, so warming water can disrupt ecosystems.
kps.core: Aquatic organisms often thrive within specific temperature ranges, so warming water can disrupt ecosystems.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how species movement to cooler waters can affect food webs.
kps.application: Explain how species movement to cooler waters can affect food webs.

kps.title: KP4: Coral bleaching
kps.coreHtml: Warming oceans can cause corals to expel algae that provide food, leading to coral bleaching and death.
kps.core: Warming oceans can cause corals to expel algae that provide food, leading to coral bleaching and death.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect ocean temperature change to damage in reef habitats.
kps.application: Connect ocean temperature change to damage in reef habitats.

kps.title: KP5: Food-web disruption
kps.coreHtml: When prey species move because of warming waters, predators may lose reliable food sources.
kps.core: When prey species move because of warming waters, predators may lose reliable food sources.
kps.applicationHtml: Use the tufted puffin example to explain how warming ocean water can reduce survival of young animals.
kps.application: Use the tufted puffin example to explain how warming ocean water can reduce survival of young animals.

kps.titleHtml: KP6: Radiation exposure in terrestrial ecosystems
kps.title: KP6: Radiation exposure in terrestrial ecosystems
kps.coreHtml: Terrestrial ecosystems can be strongly harmed by radioactive materials because these materials can accumulate in soil.
kps.core: Terrestrial ecosystems can be strongly harmed by radioactive materials because these materials can accumulate in soil.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why radiation exposure may persist longer on land than in aquatic systems.
kps.application: Explain why radiation exposure may persist longer on land than in aquatic systems.

kps.titleHtml: KP7: Nuclear accidents and ecosystem damage
kps.title: KP7: Nuclear accidents and ecosystem damage
kps.coreHtml: Radiation from nuclear accidents can damage organisms, cause mutations, and disrupt decomposition and nutrient cycling.
kps.core: Radiation from nuclear accidents can damage organisms, cause mutations, and disrupt decomposition and nutrient cycling.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect Fukushima and Chernobyl examples to long-term ecosystem effects.
kps.application: Connect Fukushima and Chernobyl examples to long-term ecosystem effects.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: Storing Nuclear Waste Safely — p.254
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Storing Nuclear Waste Safely — p.254

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Nuclear waste and radioactive decay
kps.title: KP1: Nuclear waste and radioactive decay
kps.coreHtml: Nuclear wastes contain radioactive isotopes that release harmful radiation through nuclear decay over time.
kps.core: Nuclear wastes contain radioactive isotopes that release harmful radiation through nuclear decay over time.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why some nuclear waste remains hazardous for a long time.
kps.application: Explain why some nuclear waste remains hazardous for a long time.

kps.title: KP2: Nuclear waste classification
kps.coreHtml: Nuclear waste is classified by how much containment and isolation it requires.
kps.core: Nuclear waste is classified by how much containment and isolation it requires.
kps.applicationHtml: Distinguish low-level, intermediate-level, and high-level waste by storage needs.
kps.application: Distinguish low-level, intermediate-level, and high-level waste by storage needs.

kps.title: KP3: High-level waste storage
kps.coreHtml: Used nuclear fuel gives off radiation and thermal energy, so it is cooled under water before being stored in concrete containers.
kps.core: Used nuclear fuel gives off radiation and thermal energy, so it is cooled under water before being stored in concrete containers.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why nuclear waste storage must manage both radiation and heat.
kps.application: Explain why nuclear waste storage must manage both radiation and heat.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: The Not So Silent Sea — p.255
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: The Not So Silent Sea — p.255

kps.title: KP1: Underwater noise pollution
kps.coreHtml: Marine vessels can produce underwater noise pollution in busy shipping routes.
kps.core: Marine vessels can produce underwater noise pollution in busy shipping routes.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect tankers and freighters in the Salish Sea to increased underwater sound energy.
kps.application: Connect tankers and freighters in the Salish Sea to increased underwater sound energy.

kps.title: KP2: Effects on orcas
kps.coreHtml: Noise pollution can interfere with orcas’ communication, food-finding, and stress levels.
kps.core: Noise pollution can interfere with orcas’ communication, food-finding, and stress levels.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why sound energy can become an ecosystem problem.
kps.application: Explain why sound energy can become an ecosystem problem.

kps.title: KP3: Reducing noise impacts
kps.coreHtml: Engine and propeller noise can be reduced through design and regulation.
kps.core: Engine and propeller noise can be reduced through design and regulation.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate whether noise pollution should be managed to protect endangered marine animals.
kps.application: Evaluate whether noise pollution should be managed to protect endangered marine animals.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Be Inspired — Marilyn Slett: Advocating for an Indigenous Marine Response Centre — p.256
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Be Inspired — Marilyn Slett: Advocating for an Indigenous Marine Response Centre — p.256

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Oil-spill response and marine ecosystems
kps.title: KP1: Oil-spill response and marine ecosystems
kps.coreHtml: Diesel fuel and other marine contaminants can harm coastal and marine ecosystems.
kps.core: Diesel fuel and other marine contaminants can harm coastal and marine ecosystems.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect oil-spill response planning to ecosystem protection.
kps.application: Connect oil-spill response planning to ecosystem protection.

kps.titleHtml: KP2: First Peoples as first responders
kps.title: KP2: First Peoples as first responders
kps.coreHtml: First Peoples are often first responders to marine emergencies in B.C. coastal territories.
kps.core: First Peoples are often first responders to marine emergencies in B.C. coastal territories.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why local and Indigenous knowledge matters in emergency response.
kps.application: Explain why local and Indigenous knowledge matters in emergency response.

kps.title: KP3: Indigenous Marine Response Centre
kps.coreHtml: An Indigenous Marine Response Centre would combine Western science and First Peoples knowledge for faster and more effective spill response.
kps.core: An Indigenous Marine Response Centre would combine Western science and First Peoples knowledge for faster and more effective spill response.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate how community capacity and local response time can reduce harm from marine emergencies.
kps.application: Evaluate how community capacity and local response time can reduce harm from marine emergencies.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-F: Specific Heat Capacity — p.258–260
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-F: Specific Heat Capacity — p.258–260

kps.title: KP1: Comparing water and soil
kps.coreHtml: Water and soil absorb radiant energy differently and change temperature at different rates.
kps.core: Water and soil absorb radiant energy differently and change temperature at different rates.
kps.applicationHtml: Use temperature data to compare the specific heat capacity of water and soil.
kps.application: Use temperature data to compare the specific heat capacity of water and soil.

kps.title: KP2: Air temperature above surfaces
kps.coreHtml: The temperature above water and soil changes as energy is absorbed, transferred, and released.
kps.core: The temperature above water and soil changes as energy is absorbed, transferred, and released.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how land and water surfaces affect local air temperature.
kps.application: Explain how land and water surfaces affect local air temperature.

kps.title: KP3: Specific heat capacity equation
kps.coreHtml: \(Q = mc\Delta T\), where \(Q\) is heat absorbed or released, \(m\) is mass, \(c\) is specific heat capacity, and \(\Delta T\) is temperature change.
kps.core: \(Q = mc T\), where \(Q\) is heat absorbed or released, \(m\) is mass, \(c\) is specific heat capacity, and \( T\) is temperature change.
kps.applicationHtml: Calculate heat transfer or specific heat capacity using measured data.
kps.application: Calculate heat transfer or specific heat capacity using measured data.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Sea breezes and land breezes
kps.title: KP4: Sea breezes and land breezes
kps.coreHtml: Differences in heating and cooling between land and water can create local winds.
kps.core: Differences in heating and cooling between land and water can create local winds.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain daytime sea breezes and nighttime land breezes using specific heat capacity.
kps.application: Explain daytime sea breezes and nighttime land breezes using specific heat capacity.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-G: Modelling Changes in Earth’s Systems — p.261–263
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-G: Modelling Changes in Earth’s Systems — p.261–263

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Physical models of Earth systems
kps.title: KP1: Physical models of Earth systems
kps.coreHtml: Physical models can represent parts of complex Earth systems.
kps.core: Physical models can represent parts of complex Earth systems.
kps.applicationHtml: Build and evaluate a model of the natural greenhouse effect.
kps.application: Build and evaluate a model of the natural greenhouse effect.

kps.titleHtml: KP2: Natural and human-induced greenhouse effects
kps.title: KP2: Natural and human-induced greenhouse effects
kps.coreHtml: A greenhouse-effect model should allow light in, absorb energy, transform it to thermal energy, and limit infrared radiation leaving the system.
kps.core: A greenhouse-effect model should allow light in, absorb energy, transform it to thermal energy, and limit infrared radiation leaving the system.
kps.applicationHtml: Adjust variables to model how human-induced changes can affect atmospheric temperature.
kps.application: Adjust variables to model how human-induced changes can affect atmospheric temperature.

kps.title: KP3: Ocean carbon dioxide absorption
kps.coreHtml: Oceans absorb \(CO_2\), which can react with water to form carbonic acid.
kps.core: Oceans absorb \(CO 2\), which can react with water to form carbonic acid.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect atmospheric \(CO_2\) absorption to increasing ocean acidity.
kps.application: Connect atmospheric \(CO 2\) absorption to increasing ocean acidity.

kps.title: KP4: Ocean acidity and shells
kps.coreHtml: Increased acidity can make it harder for organisms such as corals, clams, and sea urchins to build shells or skeletons.
kps.core: Increased acidity can make it harder for organisms such as corals, clams, and sea urchins to build shells or skeletons.
kps.applicationHtml: Use eggshell models to test how acidity affects calcium-containing shells.
kps.application: Use eggshell models to test how acidity affects calcium-containing shells.

kps.title: KP5: Model strengths and limitations
kps.coreHtml: Models can reveal patterns but may simplify real Earth systems.
kps.core: Models can reveal patterns but may simplify real Earth systems.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate which parts of a greenhouse or ocean-acidity model are accurate and which are incomplete.
kps.application: Evaluate which parts of a greenhouse or ocean-acidity model are accurate and which are incomplete.

chapters.titleHtml: Chapter 3.4: How does energy transformation affect humans?
chapters.title: Chapter 3.4: How does energy transformation affect humans?

concepts.titleHtml: 3.4 Concept 1: Energy transformations provide benefits, but not without risk of harm — p.266–267
concepts.title: 3.4 Concept 1: Energy transformations provide benefits, but not without risk of harm — p.266–267

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Human reliance on energy transformations
kps.title: KP1: Human reliance on energy transformations
kps.coreHtml: Human societies use technologies and understandings to benefit from energy transformations.
kps.core: Human societies use technologies and understandings to benefit from energy transformations.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify both benefits and risks when evaluating a human use of energy.
kps.application: Identify both benefits and risks when evaluating a human use of energy.

kps.title: KP2: Biological energy transformations
kps.coreHtml: Plants transform solar energy into chemical potential energy, and cellular respiration transforms that energy for life functions.
kps.core: Plants transform solar energy into chemical potential energy, and cellular respiration transforms that energy for life functions.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect human survival to photosynthesis and cellular respiration.
kps.application: Connect human survival to photosynthesis and cellular respiration.

kps.titleHtml: KP3: Fire and early human technology
kps.title: KP3: Fire and early human technology
kps.coreHtml: Fire-starting transformed chemical potential energy in wood into thermal and radiant energy.
kps.core: Fire-starting transformed chemical potential energy in wood into thermal and radiant energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how fire supported cooking, lighting, and survival in colder climates.
kps.application: Explain how fire supported cooking, lighting, and survival in colder climates.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Large-scale combustion and air pollution
kps.title: KP4: Large-scale combustion and air pollution
kps.coreHtml: Burning wood, coal, and fossil fuels transforms chemical potential energy but can create pollution and health problems.
kps.core: Burning wood, coal, and fossil fuels transforms chemical potential energy but can create pollution and health problems.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect steam engines, gas engines, smog, and air pollution to combustion-related energy transformations.
kps.application: Connect steam engines, gas engines, smog, and air pollution to combustion-related energy transformations.

kps.title: KP5: Cement and batteries
kps.coreHtml: Cement production and batteries provide major benefits but can also create greenhouse gas emissions or toxic waste.
kps.core: Cement production and batteries provide major benefits but can also create greenhouse gas emissions or toxic waste.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate why useful energy technologies can still produce environmental harm.
kps.application: Evaluate why useful energy technologies can still produce environmental harm.

kps.title: KP6: Hydroelectric dams
kps.coreHtml: Hydroelectric dams transform gravitational potential and mechanical kinetic energy into electrical energy.
kps.core: Hydroelectric dams transform gravitational potential and mechanical kinetic energy into electrical energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare the benefit of electrical energy with harms such as land flooding, methane release, and displacement of people.
kps.application: Compare the benefit of electrical energy with harms such as land flooding, methane release, and displacement of people.

kps.titleHtml: KP7: Nuclear reactors and fuel cells
kps.title: KP7: Nuclear reactors and fuel cells
kps.coreHtml: Nuclear reactors and fuel cells can provide useful energy, but each has risks or hidden impacts.
kps.core: Nuclear reactors and fuel cells can provide useful energy, but each has risks or hidden impacts.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare nuclear medical and electrical uses with radiation risk, and fuel cell benefits with hydrogen-production emissions.
kps.application: Compare nuclear medical and electrical uses with radiation risk, and fuel cell benefits with hydrogen-production emissions.

concepts.titleHtml: 3.4 Concept 2: We take action and create technology to reduce the harmful impacts of energy transformation — p.268–269
concepts.title: 3.4 Concept 2: We take action and create technology to reduce the harmful impacts of energy transformation — p.268–269

kps.title: KP1: Personal and national action
kps.coreHtml: Reducing harmful impacts of technology-related energy transformation requires individual and collective choices.
kps.core: Reducing harmful impacts of technology-related energy transformation requires individual and collective choices.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify actions such as reducing energy use and choosing sustainable technologies.
kps.application: Identify actions such as reducing energy use and choosing sustainable technologies.

kps.title: KP2: Trade-offs in action
kps.coreHtml: Reducing harm may require commitment and sacrifices in comfort or convenience.
kps.core: Reducing harm may require commitment and sacrifices in comfort or convenience.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate energy decisions by considering both benefit and sacrifice.
kps.application: Evaluate energy decisions by considering both benefit and sacrifice.

kps.title: KP3: Student and community initiatives
kps.coreHtml: Local actions can reduce energy use and support sustainable energy-transformation technologies.
kps.core: Local actions can reduce energy use and support sustainable energy-transformation technologies.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect cycling, recycling, and urban farming to lower pollution or reduced manufacturing impact.
kps.application: Connect cycling, recycling, and urban farming to lower pollution or reduced manufacturing impact.

kps.title: KP4: Technology is not problem-free
kps.coreHtml: Emerging technologies can reduce harm, but no technology is a complete cure-all.
kps.core: Emerging technologies can reduce harm, but no technology is a complete cure-all.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate electric vehicles by considering both tailpipe emissions and electricity generation or battery disposal.
kps.application: Evaluate electric vehicles by considering both tailpipe emissions and electricity generation or battery disposal.

kps.title: KP5: Nuclear fusion technology
kps.coreHtml: Controlled nuclear fusion could generate electrical energy, but it requires extremely high temperatures and containment.
kps.core: Controlled nuclear fusion could generate electrical energy, but it requires extremely high temperatures and containment.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why tokamak reactors use magnetic fields to contain super-heated fusion reactions.
kps.application: Explain why tokamak reactors use magnetic fields to contain super-heated fusion reactions.

kps.title: KP6: Methane cracking and hydrogen
kps.coreHtml: Methane cracking can produce hydrogen with much lower carbon dioxide emissions than steam methane reforming.
kps.core: Methane cracking can produce hydrogen with much lower carbon dioxide emissions than steam methane reforming.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate hydrogen fuel cells by considering both use-stage emissions and hydrogen production.
kps.application: Evaluate hydrogen fuel cells by considering both use-stage emissions and hydrogen production.

kps.title: KP7: Lower-emission cement
kps.coreHtml: New cement technologies aim to reduce carbon dioxide emissions or absorb carbon dioxide as cement hardens.
kps.core: New cement technologies aim to reduce carbon dioxide emissions or absorb carbon dioxide as cement hardens.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why cement production is an important target for reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
kps.application: Explain why cement production is an important target for reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

concepts.titleHtml: 3.4 Concept 3: We learn from our mistakes and each other to reduce harmful impacts of energy transformation — p.270–271
concepts.title: 3.4 Concept 3: We learn from our mistakes and each other to reduce harmful impacts of energy transformation — p.270–271

kps.title: KP1: Learning from past mistakes
kps.coreHtml: Societies can reduce harm by using new knowledge and changed perspectives to improve decisions.
kps.core: Societies can reduce harm by using new knowledge and changed perspectives to improve decisions.
kps.applicationHtml: Analyze past energy-related harms to identify safer future choices.
kps.application: Analyze past energy-related harms to identify safer future choices.

kps.title: KP2: Fukushima Daiichi accident
kps.coreHtml: The Fukushima accident involved an earthquake, tsunami, power loss, disabled backup generators, reactor core meltdowns, explosions, and escaped radioactive materials.
kps.core: The Fukushima accident involved an earthquake, tsunami, power loss, disabled backup generators, reactor core meltdowns, explosions, and escaped radioactive materials.
kps.applicationHtml: Use the accident to explain how system failures can multiply during energy disasters.
kps.application: Use the accident to explain how system failures can multiply during energy disasters.

kps.title: KP3: Radiation exposure and cleanup
kps.coreHtml: Nuclear accidents can expose people and environments to harmful radioactive materials.
kps.core: Nuclear accidents can expose people and environments to harmful radioactive materials.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect cleanup efforts and public safety planning to radiation risk.
kps.application: Connect cleanup efforts and public safety planning to radiation risk.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Safety inspections and technology redesign
kps.title: KP4: Safety inspections and technology redesign
kps.coreHtml: After accidents, governments and engineers can inspect existing systems and improve technology to reduce future risk.
kps.core: After accidents, governments and engineers can inspect existing systems and improve technology to reduce future risk.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how older reactor designs can be reassessed after a major failure.
kps.application: Explain how older reactor designs can be reassessed after a major failure.

kps.title: KP5: Different national responses
kps.coreHtml: Countries may respond differently to the same accident, from reducing nuclear power use to developing safer nuclear technologies.
kps.core: Countries may respond differently to the same accident, from reducing nuclear power use to developing safer nuclear technologies.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare energy policy decisions by safety, reliability, climate impact, and public trust.
kps.application: Compare energy policy decisions by safety, reliability, climate impact, and public trust.

kps.title: KP6: Managing used nuclear fuel
kps.coreHtml: Used nuclear fuel must be managed and stored so that it does not harm life or land.
kps.core: Used nuclear fuel must be managed and stored so that it does not harm life or land.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why nuclear energy decisions must include long-term waste management.
kps.application: Explain why nuclear energy decisions must include long-term waste management.

kps.titleHtml: KP7: Indigenous knowledge and Western science
kps.title: KP7: Indigenous knowledge and Western science
kps.coreHtml: Nuclear waste planning can include collaboration between Indigenous knowledge and Western science.
kps.core: Nuclear waste planning can include collaboration between Indigenous knowledge and Western science.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate why storage-site decisions should include local communities, First Nations, Inuit, and Métis perspectives.
kps.application: Evaluate why storage-site decisions should include local communities, First Nations, Inuit, and Métis perspectives.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take a Stand — Should we use nuclear power? — p.272
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take a Stand — Should we use nuclear power? — p.272

kps.title: KP1: Nuclear power concerns
kps.coreHtml: Public opinion about nuclear power is shaped by accident risk and nuclear waste risk.
kps.core: Public opinion about nuclear power is shaped by accident risk and nuclear waste risk.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify major arguments against nuclear power.
kps.application: Identify major arguments against nuclear power.

kps.title: KP2: Nuclear power benefits
kps.coreHtml: Nuclear power can be viewed as dependable, reduce dependence on energy imports, and contribute less to climate change than fossil fuels.
kps.core: Nuclear power can be viewed as dependable, reduce dependence on energy imports, and contribute less to climate change than fossil fuels.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify major arguments in favour of nuclear power.
kps.application: Identify major arguments in favour of nuclear power.

kps.title: KP3: Fukushima and public trust
kps.coreHtml: Fukushima changed public perception by raising concern about accident management, slow information release, and institutional trust.
kps.core: Fukushima changed public perception by raising concern about accident management, slow information release, and institutional trust.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why energy decisions depend on both technical evidence and public confidence.
kps.application: Explain why energy decisions depend on both technical evidence and public confidence.

kps.title: KP4: Evidence-based position
kps.coreHtml: A position on nuclear power should weigh safety, cost, waste, reliability, and climate change.
kps.core: A position on nuclear power should weigh safety, cost, waste, reliability, and climate change.
kps.applicationHtml: Prepare a reasoned argument for or against nuclear power.
kps.application: Prepare a reasoned argument for or against nuclear power.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-H: Energy Transformation Debate — p.274–275
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-H: Energy Transformation Debate — p.274–275

kps.title: KP1: Debate question
kps.coreHtml: The central question is whether technology-based energy transformations benefit society more than they harm it.
kps.core: The central question is whether technology-based energy transformations benefit society more than they harm it.
kps.applicationHtml: Choose an energy transformation and evaluate both benefits and harms.
kps.application: Choose an energy transformation and evaluate both benefits and harms.

kps.title: KP2: Research planning
kps.coreHtml: A debate requires clear research questions, reliable resources, organized findings, and cited evidence.
kps.core: A debate requires clear research questions, reliable resources, organized findings, and cited evidence.
kps.applicationHtml: Build an argument using evidence about energy transformations in transportation, electricity, agriculture, manufacturing, medicine, or food systems.
kps.application: Build an argument using evidence about energy transformations in transportation, electricity, agriculture, manufacturing, medicine, or food systems.

kps.title: KP3: Bias and reliability
kps.coreHtml: Resources should be assessed for political position, special interests, funding, evidence, citations, balance, and emotional appeal.
kps.core: Resources should be assessed for political position, special interests, funding, evidence, citations, balance, and emotional appeal.
kps.applicationHtml: Distinguish reliable evidence from biased or one-sided claims.
kps.application: Distinguish reliable evidence from biased or one-sided claims.

kps.title: KP4: Evidence-based communication
kps.coreHtml: A strong energy debate uses facts, logic, and clear reasoning rather than unsupported opinions.
kps.core: A strong energy debate uses facts, logic, and clear reasoning rather than unsupported opinions.
kps.applicationHtml: Present a balanced argument and evaluate which side is better supported.
kps.application: Present a balanced argument and evaluate which side is better supported.

summaryHtml:
Unit Summary

- Total Chapters: 4

- Total Core Sections: 14

- Total Core Knowledge Points: 94

- Optional Extension / Investigation Sections: 16

- Optional Extension / Investigation Knowledge Points: 53

- Top 5 Core Formulas:

• Mechanical kinetic energy: \(E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\)

• Gravitational potential energy: \(E_g = mg\Delta h\)

• Cellular respiration: \(C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{energy}\)

• Photosynthesis: \(6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{energy} \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2\)

• Law of conservation of energy: energy is transferred or transformed, not created or destroyed.

- Top 5 Common Error Areas:

• Confusing energy transfer with energy transformation.

• Treating “lost” energy as destroyed instead of transformed into less useful forms such as thermal or sound energy.

• Confusing chemical bond energy transformations with nuclear energy transformations.

• Confusing radiation, conduction, convection, greenhouse gases, and greenhouse effect.

• Listing only benefits of energy technologies without evaluating risks, waste, safety, and public trust.

- Suggested Learning Order:

• Start with basic energy properties: system, surroundings, forms of energy, transfer, transformation, and conservation.

• Study mechanical energy formulas: mechanical kinetic energy, gravitational potential energy, variables, reference position, and friction.

• Study energy transformations in chemical reactions, cellular respiration, photosynthesis, fossil fuels, and fuel cells.

• Study nuclear energy and light-energy transformations: decay, radiation, fission, fusion, absorption, chlorophyll, photovoltaic cells, and vision.

• Study Earth as an energy system: solar energy, gravitational energy, nuclear energy, radiation, greenhouse gases, conduction, convection, water cycle, and specific heat capacity.

• Finish with human impacts: energy technology benefits, pollution, dams, nuclear accidents, sustainable technologies, and long-term waste decisions.

- Suggested Review Order:

• Review energy vocabulary first: system, surroundings, kinetic, potential, transfer, transformation, open system, closed system, isolated system.

• Practise \(E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\) and \(E_g = mg\Delta h\), including variable effects and reference position.

• Compare exothermic/endothermic reactions, cellular respiration/photosynthesis, and fuel cells/fossil fuels.

• Compare chemical reactions, nuclear decay, fission, fusion, and light-energy absorption.

• Review Earth systems by tracing solar energy from radiation to atmosphere heating, water cycle movement, and ecosystem effects.

• Review human energy decisions by sorting each technology into benefit, risk, harm-reduction strategy, and unresolved issue.
